nativeRange.habitat: Shallow fore-reef slopes, sheltered lagoons, and back-reef areas; most abundant in lagoonal environments where colonies can dominate substrate coverage. Branch thickness varies with current strength.

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae. Supplemental feeding of fine-particle foods such as rotifers, micro-plankton, and vitamin-enriched coral foods on a weekly basis has been reported to support growth and coloration, though it is generally considered optional for established colonies.

goodWith: other SPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: aggressive LPS corals with long sweeper tentacles
zoanthids allowed to spread into contact
physically rough fish that may break branches

summary: Seriatopora (Birdsnest Coral) is an SPS coral in family Pocilloporidae, prized for its distinctive thin, needle-like branches. Considered more forgiving than Acropora; intermediate-level care with moderate-high lighting and high flow.
careNotes:
Seriatopora is a genus of 9 recognized species in family Pocilloporidae, closely related to Pocillopora and Stylophora. In the aquarium trade the most commonly encountered species are S. hystrix (thin, needle-like branches) and S. caliendrum (slightly thicker branches with a more open structure). Both are sold under the trade name "Birdsnest Coral" and share near-identical husbandry requirements.

Morphology: colonies form dense, tangled bushes of sharply-tapering needle-like branches — the defining "birdsnest" appearance. Polyps are arranged in orderly rows and extend only at night. Coloration in the trade spans cream, pink, green, yellow, and purple, with some aquacultured designer morphs bearing contrasting polyp colors (e.g., "Purple Polyp Green Birdsnest").

Key care requirements:
• Lighting: Moderate to high intensity. Sources consistently recommend 150–300 PAR; acclimate new specimens by starting lower in the tank and raising over 2–3 weeks to prevent bleaching.
• Flow: High, indirect, turbulent (non-laminar) flow is generally advised. Direct laminar blasts can cause tissue damage. As colonies grow denser, flow penetration becomes critical; random gyre-type flow is preferred. Branch thickness tends to be greater in higher-flow environments.
• Water chemistry: Calcium, alkalinity, and magnesium should be maintained stably. Seriatopora consumes alkalinity rapidly during growth phases. Sudden alkalinity swings have been associated with RTN/STN onset — stability is more important than hitting any precise target.
• RTN/STN risk: While more forgiving than Acropora, Seriatopora is known in the hobby to be susceptible to Rapid Tissue Necrosis (RTN) and Slow Tissue Necrosis (STN), particularly in response to sudden parameter swings, physical damage to the delicate branches, or shipping stress. Dip new frags upon introduction.
• Placement: Upper to mid-column placement is generally recommended to provide adequate light and flow penetration.
• Aggression: Peaceful — does not deploy sweeper tentacles, but physical contact with neighboring corals should be prevented as with all SPS.
• Growth: Fast-growing for an SPS coral; colonies can achieve significant size within months under ideal conditions. In the wild, colonies can exceed 1 meter across; typical aquarium colonies reach 10–30 cm.
• Fragging: Among the easiest SPS corals to propagate. Clean cuts with bone cutters or scissors; frags glued on their side reportedly establish faster. IUCN status of S. hystrix is Vulnerable despite being relatively common — aquacultured specimens are preferred.
• Care level: Sources vary between beginner and intermediate. Easier than Acropora due to greater tolerance for minor parameter variation, but susceptibility to RTN/STN and sensitivity to physical damage of the thin branches warrants intermediate classification.
• Cross-source notes: Temperature range reflects averaged consensus across LiveAquaria (72–78°F), AquariumBreeder (74–83°F), and Reefchasers (77–84°F); min 23°C, max 27°C. PAR range 150–300 reflects the middle ground between Extreme Corals (150–300) and ReefChasers (200–400).
breedingNotes: Fragging is the primary propagation method and is among the simplest of any SPS coral. Colonies can be cut with bone cutters or sharp scissors; frags as small as 1–2 cm will encrust and grow on frag plugs or rubble. Gluing frags on their side is reported to encourage faster lateral branching. Sexual reproduction occurs via broadcast spawning on natural reefs but is not practiced in captivity.

additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (77–84°F), pH (8.1–8.6), alkalinity (8–9 dKH), calcium (430–460 ppm), PAR (200–400). Care level listed as BEGINNER.
